title: IO3 Methodological Guide for the Development of Collaborative Projects among Rural Youth
author: Regueira-Ces, María
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This record preserves the multilingual versions of the project deliverable IO3, “Methodological Guide for the Development of Collaborative Projects among Rural Youth”, produced within the Erasmus+ project REC-THINK / RECTHINK (Project No. 2015-2-ES02-KA205-006640), “Fight against isolation through participative IT tools: the participatory video”. 
 The guide was developed to support organisations, youth workers, facilitators and community actors interested in promoting the participation and active involvement of young people in rural areas through collaborative, community-based and transformative processes. The Spanish version is the original version of the guide. The English, Portuguese, French and Italian files are the multilingual versions disseminated within the project framework. 
 The publication examines the social and territorial context affecting rural youth in Europe, with particular attention to depopulation, ageing, migration, unequal access to services, internet connectivity and the limited presence of young people in decision-making processes. On this basis, it proposes a practical and methodological approach to collaborative projects as tools for participation, collective intelligence, non-formal education and community development. 
 The guide combines contextual analysis with practical orientation. It addresses key elements for the design and implementation of collaborative projects, including youth participation, facilitation, co-creation, community engagement, shared responsibility, goal setting, implementation and evaluation. It also presents a selection of digital tools for communication and collaboration, together with face-to-face methodologies adapted to rural contexts. 
 A central section of the guide introduces the Oasis Game as a participatory methodology for identifying community assets, building collective visions, co-creating local solutions, carrying out community action and sustaining future cycles of participation and learning. 
 This output was developed in 2016 and should therefore be understood in the methodological, technological and policy context of that period. While its participatory and community-based approach remains relevant, some contextual references, digital tools and examples reflect the conditions in which the guide was originally produced. 
 This output was created within the REC-THINK partnership and is deposited here for preservation, open dissemination and citation. According to the original publication, the guide was released under a Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) licence.
